  • Portal Particle Acceleration In Jets

Particle acceleration at recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets

Paper Details

Authors:E. Peretti, E. Amato, S. S. Cerri, G. Morlino, L.P. Pullano, S. Recchia
arXiv ID:2603.16647
Submitted:2026-03-18
Key concepts: Recollimation shocks, DSA, Jet-cocoon systems, Astrophysical jets

Abstract

Growing observational evidence suggests that sub-relativistic astrophysical jets may accelerate particles at slowly evolving standing shocks. Recollimation shocks are expected to develop when jets expand in dense environments; their formation may be mediated by the pressure of the cocoon surrounding the jet, while remaining compatible with a quasi-stationary behavior. Despite their high inclination relative to the jet axis, such shocks can be strong and enable efficient particle acceleration. This work develops a versatile modeling framework for particle acceleration via diffusive shock acceleration at recollimation shocks, applicable to Seyfert galaxies, microquasars, and protostellar systems.

Key findings

  • Recollimation shocks as DSA sites — Recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets are quasi-stationary and strong enough to be plausible sites for diffusive shock acceleration1.
  • Semi-analytic framework — A 1D transport model coupled to an extended Bromberg et al. (2011) hydrodynamic framework predicts particle spectra and maximum energies for three jet classes1.
  • Maximum proton energies — Predicted Emax: Seyferts up to PeV–EeV (depending on turbulence regime), microquasars up to tens of PeV, protostellar jets up to TeV1.
  • Diffusion regime matters — The turbulence spectrum (Bohm, Kraichnan, or Kolmogorov) strongly shapes the cutoff energy and spectral form; Bohm gives highest Emax, Kolmogorov the lowest1.
  • Upstream confinement — Upstream diffusion length relative to shock position typically sets Emax, though downstream turbulence decay can become the limiting factor1.

Method

The authors extend the analytic jet–cocoon formalism of Bromberg et al. (2011) to the sub-relativistic regime, deriving cocoon pressure Pc(t), jet-head position zh(t), and recollimation-shock height. Self-similar scalings (analogous to Weaver 1977 wind-blown bubbles) yield Pc ~ t-4/5. Oblique shock jump conditions are parameterized via kp, and a semi-analytic 1D spatially-dependent transport equation is solved iteratively to obtain particle spectra at the shock1, 2.

Results by source class

Seyfert jets (SEY0)

Using NGC 4151–inspired parameters, the model predicts proton acceleration up to PeV–EeV energies under Bohm diffusion, with Kraichnan as a realistic benchmark yielding Emax ~ 1017–1018 eV1, 3.

Microquasar jets (MQ0)

SS433-inspired prototype parameters yield Emax up to tens of PeV, consistent with recent HAWC and H.E.S.S. detections of TeV emission from SS4331, 4, 5.

Protostellar jets (PS0)

HH80-81–based parameters predict proton energies reaching TeV under Kraichnan diffusion, with Kolmogorov yielding only GeV-scale acceleration1, 6.

Significance

This paper provides the first unified semi-analytic framework for DSA at recollimation shocks across three classes of sub-relativistic jets. It demonstrates that recollimation shocks — previously understudied as particle accelerators — can be highly efficient, with implications for PeVatron candidates among microquasars and Seyferts. The work also highlights that escaped protons may produce extended hadronic emission from jet cocoons, connecting to multi-messenger predictions.

Limitations & future work

  • 1D model omits multi-dimensional transport effects and lateral escape
  • Oblique-shock physics (guide-field effects, Kirk 1989) not fully captured
  • Sub-relativistic only; relativistic generalization needed for fast AGN jets
  • Microphysics at jet head and downstream turbulence decay remain uncertain

Related concepts

  • Recollimation shocks — central mechanism of this paper
  • Diffusive shock acceleration — the acceleration process studied
  • Jet-cocoon systems — dynamical framework
  • Astrophysical jets — broader context
  • Shock waves — foundational physics
  • Particle acceleration basics — background
  • Oblique shock acceleration — specific geometry
  • Relativistic jets — comparison regime
  • Bromberg et al. (2011) — foundational formalism

References

  1. Peretti, E., Amato, E., Cerri, S. S., Morlino, G., Pullano, L.P., Recchia, S. (2026). "Particle acceleration at recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets." arXiv:2603.16647
  2. Bromberg, O., Nakar, E., Piran, T., Sari, R. (2011). "The propagation of relativistic jets in external media." ApJ, 740, 100. DOI
  3. Ulvestad, J. S. et al. (2005). "A sub-parsec radio jet in NGC 4151." DOI
  4. HAWC Collaboration (2018). "Very-high-energy particle acceleration from the microquasar SS 433." Nature. DOI
  5. H.E.S.S. Collaboration (2024). "Resolved TeV emission from SS 433." arXiv
  6. Rodríguez-Kamenetzky, A. et al. (2017). "The HH 80-81 jet." ApJ, 851, 16. DOI
  7. Hillas, A. M. (1984). "The Origin of Ultra-High-Energy Cosmic Rays." ARA&A, 22, 425. DOI
